You can perform various actions to move a stickman. You are given 3 labeled actions (A, B, C) each illustrated by an image. Then, given a GOAL image, determine the shortest sequence of actions (Eg. AABC) that achieves the goal state shown in the 5th image. Only use the labels A, B, C in your answer.

Examine the input images and reason what each of them means and finally come up with the shortest sequence. Your output must be a JSON object with two fields: 'reasoning' and 'sequence'. The 'sequence' field must be a string containing only the capital letters representing the sequence of actions.

This is the shortest at 2 actions.\n\nWait, but does action C keep you in place horizontally? Looking at image C again - the initial and final positions seem to have the same horizontal position. The combination would be A+C together or sequentially.\n\nIf the stickman starts at the far left and needs to reach the right side:\n- The red block is roughly in the middle\n- The black stickman ends up to the right of the red block\n\nDistance analysis:\n- Gray stickman at ~3cm\n- Red block from ~6cm to ~9cm  \n- Black stickman at ~13cm\n\nOne A action moves about 5 units.   "reasoning": "Let me analyze the actions and the goal state:\n\nAction A: The stickman moves to the RIGHT (from left position to right position)\nAction B: The stickman moves to the LEFT (from right position to left position)\nAction C: The stickman moves UP/JUMPS (from lower position to higher position - floats up)\n\nGoal state analysis:\n- There's a gray stickman on the LEFT side\n- There's a black stickman on the RIGHT side\n- There's a red obstacle/block on the ground between them, slightly left of center\n\nThe stickman starts at some initial position. Looking at the actions:\n- The gray figure represents the initial/starting position\n- The black figure represents the final/current position of the stickman\n\nIn the goal image, the black stickman is on the RIGHT side, and the gray stickman (initial position marker) is on the LEFT side. The red block is on the ground between them.\n\nThe stickman needs to be on the right side of the red block. \n\nStarting position: Let's assume the stickman starts at the left (gray position).\n\nThe red block is between the two positions. Since physics applies normally, the stickman can't walk through the red block. \n\nTo get from left to right while avoiding the red block:\n- Option 1: Jump over the red block - but Action C seems to just move up, not jump over\n- Option 2: Looking more carefully...\n\nWait, let me reconsider. The goal shows the black stickman on the right side of the red block. The stickman needs to move from its starting position to the right side without touching the red block.\n\nAction C moves the stickman upward. Action A moves to the right. \n\nIf the stickman starts on the left (where the gray figure is in the goal image), to get to the right side of the red block:\n- C (jump up) + A (move right while elevated, passing over the red block) - but that's 2 actions\n\nWait, actually looking at the goal image again:\n- Gray stickman is on the LEFT (this is the starting/initial position indicator)\n- Black stickman is on the RIGHT side of the red block\n\nSo the stickman needs to go from left to right, but there's a red block in the way on the ground.\n\nIf we just use Action A (move right), the stickman would walk into the red block (physics laws apply - can't pass through).\n\nSo we need to jump over it: C (jump up) then A (move right) - but would the stickman land on the right side?\n\nActually, looking at Action C more carefully: it moves the stickman UP significantly. And Action A moves to the right. \n\nIf we do C first (jump up), then A (move right while in the air), the stickman would be elevated and moving right, potentially clearing the red block, then landing due to gravity.\n\nBut wait - does the sequence C then A result in being on the right side? The stickman jumps up (C), then moves right (A), which should put it over/past the red block, and gravity brings it down to the right side.\n\nThat would be: CA - 2 actions\n\nAlternatively, just A alone - would the stickman simply move right and end up at the right position shown? Looking at Action A, the movement distance seems to be about 4-5 units to the right. The red block is roughly in the middle. \n\nActually, I need to reconsider the starting position. The initial position in the goal might not be the stickman's current position - it's just showing where it started before.\n\nLet me think about this differently:\n- The goal shows the black stickman on the right side of the red block, elevated slightly (it appears to be standing on the ground to the right of the red block)\n- The stickman starts somewhere and needs to reach this position\n\nIf the stickman starts at the gray position (left side), doing Action A moves it to the right. But the red block is in the way. So we need to jump (C) first to get over the red block, then move right (A).\n\nSequence: CA\n\nThis seems to be the shortest sequence (2 actions) to avoid the red obstacle and reach the right side position shown in the goal.",
  "sequence": "CA"
}

If physics apply normally, jumping while moving would carry you forward... but action C seems to just move the stickman straight up and then it comes back down in the same position due to gravity.\n\nActually, re-examining action C: It shows the stickman moving upward from ground level to a higher position. With normal physics, if you jump straight up, you land in the same spot. But you need to combine movement with jumping.\n\nLet me reconsider: Maybe A moves right one unit, B moves left one unit, and C jumps up (in place).\n\nIf the red block is at position 1-2 from start, and the goal is position 2-3 from start, the stickman needs to get past the red block.\n\nOne approach: A (move to position 1, at red block edge), then need to jump over... but if C is just jumping in place, we'd need to be clever.\n\nActually, with normal physics, if I jump (C) while at the edge of the obstacle, I would clear it if I also move (A) at the same time... but these are sequential actions.\n\nHmm, let me reconsider. Maybe C is a jump that also carries some forward momentum, or maybe the sequence needs to be: move to just before the block, jump over it (which in this puzzle might mean C clears the obstacle), then land past it.\n\nLooking at the goal image again:\n- The black stickman appears to be about 1 action A away from the right edge\n- The red block is between the gray (start) and black (goal) positions\n\nI think the simplest interpretation:\n- A = move right one step\n- B = move left one step  \n- C = jump up (in place, or with slight forward momentum)\n\nThe goal shows the stickman has moved right past the red obstacle. The obstacle is on the ground, so jumping over it would work if you jump while near it.\n\nShortest sequence to get past the red block on the right:\n- A: Move right (now at left edge of red block)\n- C: Jump (jump over the red block)\n- This might land on the right side of the red block\n\nSo the sequence could be: AC or AAC\n\nLooking at the positions in the goal image more carefully:\n- The gray stickman is at approximately position 2-3 cm on the ruler\n- The red block spans approximately position 5-9 cm\n- The black stickman is at approximately position 11-13 cm\n\nEach action A seems to move about 5-6 cm (based on the A image showing movement from ~6cm to ~12cm area).\n\nSo from start (~2cm), one A move puts stickman at ~8cm (which would be on/at the red block). Then C (jump) would clear the red block. But jumping in place would put you back at 8cm, still on the red block.\n\nMaybe with physics, you do: A (move right to ~8cm, near red block), then C (jump over block, landing at ~12cm which is past the block). This makes sense if C is treated as \"jump forward\" or if the jump naturally carries you past the obstacle.\n\nActually, I think the key insight is: the stickman needs to jump OVER the red block. In the context of this puzzle, you'd move right to approach the block, then jump to clear it. The jump (C) combined with forward momentum would carry you past the block.\n\nI'll go with: A then C (AC) - move right once, then jump to clear the red block and land on the right side.\n\nBut wait - does C provide forward movement or just upward? Looking at action C again: it shows the stickman going straight up. With normal physics, jumping straight up lands you in the same spot. So to get PAST the block, you'd need to:\n1. Jump BEFORE the block so you land on top of or past it, OR\n2. Use A to move right multiple times to get past the block without jumping\n\nActually, looking at the goal image again - the black stickman is on the RIGHT side and appears to be at GROUND LEVEL (not elevated). The red block is between the two stickmen. The stickman needs to be to the right of the red block.\n\nIf the red block is an obstacle that blocks horizontal movement (like a wall), then you'd need to jump over it. If it's just a marker, then A moves might be enough.\n\nGiven \"normal laws of physics\" and the instruction to \"avoid red,\" I think the red block is a physical obstacle on the ground. To get past it while avoiding it, the stickman would need to jump over it.\n\nThe sequence: Move right to approach (A), then jump (C) to go over the block, and the stickman lands on the other side.\n\nSo my answer is: AC\n\nBut I need to verify if one A puts the stickman at the right jumping position. Looking at the images, the starting position and the block position seem about 1 action apart, and jumping (C) would clear the block.\n\nFinal answer: AC",

The black stickman in the goal appears to be at approximately position 13-14 cm on the ruler, while the gray stickman is at about 3-4 cm.\n\nLooking at Action A: moves right by approximately 5-6 cm\nLooking at Action B: moves left by approximately 5-6 cm\n\nIn the goal state, the black stickman is on the right side. The red obstacle is between positions ~6-11 cm approximately. The black stickman appears to be at ~13 cm.\n\nIf the stickman starts in the middle (around position 9-10 cm based on the action images), and needs to be at position ~13 cm (to the right of the red block), it needs to move RIGHT once using Action A.\n\nBut wait - there's a red block to avoid. If the stickman moves right normally, would it pass through the red block? The red block is on the ground level. If physics apply normally, the stickman would need to jump over it or go around it.\n\nActually, looking more carefully at the goal - the black stickman is already to the RIGHT of the red block. The stickman needs to get to the right of the red block without touching it.\n\nIf we use Action C (jump up) and then Action A (move right), the stickman could jump over the red block.\n\nBut actually, let me reconsider the starting position. The stickman in action images appears to start at around position 9-13 cm. In the goal, the black stickman is at about 13-14 cm.\n\nWait - looking at the goal image again:\n- The gray stickman (initial/ghost position?) is on the left at ~3 cm\n- The black stickman (current position) is on the right at ~13-14 cm\n- The red block is at about 6-11 cm\n\nThe stickman seems to need to be to the right of the red block. If the stickman starts at the left (like the gray ghost shows), it needs to move right but jump over the red block.\n\nActually, the gray stickman in the goal might just be showing where the stickman started (reference position), not an obstacle.\n\nIf the stickman starts on the left and needs to get to the right of the red block:\n- Option 1: Jump (C) then move right (A) - but need to time the jump to clear the block\n- Option 2: Move right enough times then the block is to the left\n\nHmm, let me think about this differently. \n\nThe red block appears to be on the ground between positions ~6 and ~11 cm. The black stickman's final position is at ~13 cm, which is to the RIGHT of the red block.\n\nIf Action A moves right by ~5 cm:\n- Start: ~3 cm (left position where gray ghost is)\n- After A: ~8 cm (lands on/near the red block - BAD)\n- After AA: ~13 cm (past the red block - GOOD)\n\nBut with normal physics and a block on the ground, the stickman can't just walk through the block. It would need to jump over it.\n\nSo the sequence might be: A (move right to before the block), C (jump), A (land to the right of the block)?\n\nOr maybe: C (jump up), A (move right while jumping, clearing the block)?\n\nActually, looking at the images more carefully:\n\nIn the goal, the black stickman seems to have moved right from a starting position that's similar to where the gray stickman is shown (left side). The distance moved appears to be about 2 \"A\" actions worth.\n\nThe red block appears to be at ground level in the middle. To avoid the red block, the stickman needs to jump over it.\n\nMost likely sequence: A (move right to just before block), C (jump over block), which would land to the right.\n\nOr just: CА - jump while moving right to clear the block.\n\nActually, I think the simplest interpretation is:\n- The stickman needs to end up to the right of the red block\n- To get there without touching the red block, it needs to jump over it\n- Sequence: A then C? Or C then A?\n\nLooking at Action C - it moves upward only (no horizontal movement shown). So C=jump up, A=move right.\n\nIf the block is in the middle:\n1. A - move right (now at edge of or near block)\n2. C - jump up (now above block level)\n3. A - move right (now to the right of block, landing)\n\nBut that might be 3 moves. Or maybe just:\n1. C - jump up\n2. A - move right (clears the block while in air)\n\nHmm, but action C doesn't seem to include horizontal movement.\n\nLet me reconsider. Maybe the stickman can simply do AA (two right moves) and the block is passed over naturally if it's short enough, or the block just represents a gap that needs to be jumped.\n\nActually, the most natural reading: The red block is an obstacle on the ground. The stickman needs to get past it. Since physics apply, the stickman needs to jump over it. \n\nLooking at the positions - the black stickman in the goal is 2 \"A-moves\" to the right. But there's a red block in between. So doing AA would cause collision. Instead: do A to approach, C to jump, A to land on other side = ACA.\n\nBut wait - maybe just doing A once gets the stickman to the right of the red block directly if the jump happens automatically? No, that doesn't make sense.\n\nI'll go with: The stickman needs to jump over the red block. Starting position is on the left. The shortest path would be:\n- C (jump) + A (move right while in air, clearing the block) \n\nBut if C only goes up and A goes right independently...\n\nActually I think the answer is simply A - just move right once, landing to the right of the red block if the stickman starts far enough left. OR the sequence is CA or AC.\n\nLet me look at the ruler positions again:\n- In Action A: stickman moves from ~9 cm to ~14 cm (moves about 5 cm right)\n- In Action B: stickman moves from ~13 cm to ~4 cm (moves about 9 cm left)  \n- In the goal: black stickman is at ~13-14 cm, gray (initial?) at ~3 cm, red block from ~7-12 cm\n\nIf the starting position is at ~3 cm (left, where gray is), and one A-action moves ~5 cm right:\n- After A: at ~8 cm (inside the red block zone - BAD)\n- After AA: at ~13 cm (past the red block - GOOD if no collision en route)\n\nBut with physics, you can't pass through the block. So you need to jump.\n\nThe sequence is likely: CA A or A CA...\n\nActually, maybe: C jumps up enough to clear the block height, and A moves right. If done together or in sequence CAA or ACA...\n\nI'll go with ACA as my best guess:\n1. A - move right toward the block\n2. C - jump over the block  \n3. A - move right to land past the block\n\nWait, but C moves UP only, not forward. After C, the stickman is high up but same horizontal position. Then A would move right at height and land.\n\nHmm, but in the goal image the stickman is on the ground level to the right of the block. The block height seems relatively small compared to the stickman.\n\nLet me simplify: The goal shows the stickman moved 2 A-distances to the right from initial position. The red block is in between. To avoid the red block, the stickman jumps. Sequence: A, C, A seems reasonable. But maybe just jumping once while moving: if C+A can be done together...\n\nI'll go with the answer: A (move to near the block), C (jump up to clear it), A (move right past it) = ACA\n\nActually wait. Let me look at this more carefully. The gray stickman on the LEFT of the goal image - is that showing the INITIAL position? And the black stickman on the RIGHT is the FINAL/GOAL position?\n\nIf so, the stickman starts on the left (~3 cm) and needs to end up on the right (~13 cm) while avoiding the red block (~6-11 cm).\n\nTwo A-actions = 2 × 5 cm = 10 cm of rightward movement, ending at ~13 cm. But the block is in the way.\n\nTo avoid the block: Jump over it. \n\nSequence: A (move right 5 cm, now at 8 cm - edge of block), C (jump up), then land... but C just goes up, not forward. After C the stickman would be directly above the block. Then A would move right over the block.\n\nSo: A, C, A = ACA? Or just C, A, A = CAA if the jump happens first?\n\nOr maybe: The stickman can do A to get close, then C to jump up high enough, and the landing from C takes them past the block (since C shows the stickman moving up and the momentum/physics would carry them forward).\n\nI think the most logical sequence is: A (approach), C (jump over block), A (clear to the other side) = ACA\n\nBut let me reconsider if the shortest could be just 2 moves. Could CА work?\n- C: jump up from starting position (~3 cm)\n- A: move right 5 cm while at height, landing at ~8 cm... which might still be on the block.\n\nOr AA if the block doesn't actually block movement (just needs to be avoided by jumping over at the right time)?\n\nI'll go with my best judgment: The sequence is A, C, A = ACA to jump over the red obstacle and land to the right of it.",
  "sequence": "ACA"
}

You can perform various actions to move a stickman. You are given 3 labeled actions (A, B, C) each illustrated by an image. Then, given a GOAL image, determine the shortest sequence of actions (Eg. AABC) that achieves the goal state shown in the 5th image. Only use the labels A, B, C in your answer. 

You can perform various actions to move a stickman. You are given 3 labeled actions (A, B, C) each illustrated by an image. 
                    Then, given a GOAL image, determine the shortest sequence of actions (Eg. AABC) that achieves the goal state shown in the 5th image.
                Only use the labels A, B, C in your answer.

Looking at the actions, the initial position seems to be somewhere in the middle or left.\n\nLooking at the goal image more carefully:\n- The gray figure appears to be the \"initial/ghost\" position on the left\n- The black figure is the current position on the right\n- The red block is between them on the ground\n\nSo the stickman needs to move from the left position to the right position, which is to the right of the red block.\n\nThe red block is on the ground between the two positions. To get from left to right while avoiding the red block, the stickman needs to jump over it.\n\nSo the sequence would be:\n- Action A: Move right (but this might go into the red block)\n- Action C: Jump up (to clear the red block)\n- Action A: Move right (land on the other side)\n\nWait, let me reconsider. Looking at the positions:\n\nThe gray stickman is on the left (initial position).\nThe black stickman is on the right (goal position).\nThe red block is on the ground between them.\n\nTo move from left to right while avoiding the red block on the ground, the stickman would need to:\n1. Jump (C) - to get over the red block\n2. Move right (A) - to land on the right side\n\nOr possibly: A (move right toward block), C (jump over block), A (continue right)\n\nActually, looking at the distances:\n- The stickman needs to cross from left to right\n- The red block is on the ground in between\n\nWith physics, jumping (C) lifts the stickman up, and then moving right (A) would bring them over. But we need to think about how many steps.\n\nThe simplest interpretation: The stickman needs to go right while avoiding the ground-level red block. \n\nIf Action A moves right by a certain distance and Action C jumps up (and presumably forward with physics, or just up), we need to figure out how to get past the block.\n\nLooking at Action C more carefully: it shows the stickman moving straight UP (the arrow points up), so it's a vertical jump, not a forward jump.\n\nSo to get from left to right past the red block:\n- C (jump up) + A (move right while in air or after landing past block)\n\nOr: A (move right to edge of block) + C (jump up) + A (move right past block)\n\nGiven the positions in the goal image, the stickman seems to have moved a significant distance to the right. \n\nLooking at the ruler in the images:\n- Action A seems to move about 5-6 cm to the right\n- Action B seems to move about 5-6 cm to the left\n- Action C moves up\n\nThe red block appears to be roughly in the middle of the image, and the black stickman is to the right of it.\n\nStarting from an initial position (center or where the gray stickman is), the stickman needs to:\n1. Move right past the red block\n\nThe simplest sequence that gets the stickman to the right of the red block while avoiding it would be:\n- C (jump) + A (move right) = jump over the block while moving right\n\nOr just A (if the block is on ground level and action A moves at ground level, it would hit the block).\n\nSince we need to avoid the red block which is on the ground, and action C jumps up, the sequence might be:\nCA - jump up then move right (landing past the block)\n\nor CА - just these two\n\nActually, thinking about it more simply: The goal shows the stickman to the RIGHT of the red block. The stickman needs to get there from the LEFT without touching the red block.\n\nThe shortest path: Jump (C) over the block, then the stickman would naturally land. But does the stickman move forward during a jump? If not, then we need A to move right.\n\nI think the sequence is: C (jump up and over) combined with A (move right) = CA\n\nBut if the jump is purely vertical, we might need: A (move right to block) then C (jump) then A (move right past block) = ACA\n\nLooking at the goal image again - the black stickman appears to be about 2 \"A-steps\" to the right of the initial position. The red block is in between.\n\nI'll go with CA as the shortest sequence - jump and then move right, or the jump carries forward momentum.",

Move right (A) - to land on the right side\\n\\nOr possibly: A (move right toward block), C (jump over block), A (continue right)\\n\\nActually, looking at the distances:\\n- The stickman needs to cross from left to right\\n- The red block is on the ground in between\\n\\nWith physics, jumping (C) lifts the stickman up, and then moving right (A) would bring them over. But we need to think about how many steps.\\n\\nThe simplest interpretation: The stickman needs to go right while avoiding the ground-level red block. \\n\\nIf Action A moves right by a certain distance and Action C jumps up (and presumably forward with physics, or just up), we need to figure out how to get past the block.\\n\\nLooking at Action C more carefully: it shows the stickman moving straight UP (the arrow points up), so it's a vertical jump, not a forward jump.\\n\\nSo to get from left to right past the red block:\\n- C (jump up) + A (move right while in air or after landing past block)\\n\\nOr: A (move right to edge of block) + C (jump up) + A (move right past block)\\n\\nGiven the positions in the goal image, the stickman seems to have moved a significant distance to the right. \\n\\nLooking at the ruler in the images:\\n- Action A seems to move about 5-6 cm to the right\\n- Action B seems to move about 5-6 cm to the left\\n- Action C moves up\\n\\nThe red block appears to be roughly in the middle of the image, and the black stickman is to the right of it.\\n\\nStarting from an initial position (center or where the gray stickman is), the stickman needs to:\\n1. Move right past the red block\\n\\nThe simplest sequence that gets the stickman to the right of the red block while avoiding it would be:\\n- C (jump) + A (move right) = jump over the block while moving right\\n\\nOr just A (if the block is on ground level and action A moves at ground level, it would hit the block).\\n\\nSince we need to avoid the red block which is on the ground, and action C jumps up, the sequence might be:\\nCA - jump up then move right (landing past the block)\\n\\nor C\u0410 - just these two\\n\\nActually, thinking about it more simply: The goal shows the stickman to the RIGHT of the red block. The stickman needs to get there from the LEFT without touching the red block.\\n\\nThe shortest path: Jump (C) over the block, then the stickman would naturally land. But does the stickman move forward during a jump? If not, then we need A to move right.\\n\\nI think the sequence is: C (jump up and over) combined with A (move right) = CA\\n\\nBut if the jump is purely vertical, we might need: A (move right to block) then C (jump) then A (move right past block) = ACA\\n\\nLooking at the goal image again - the black stickman appears to be about 2 \\\"A-steps\\\" to the right of the initial position. The red block is in between.\\n\\nI'll go with CA as the shortest sequence - jump and then move right, or the jump carries forward momentum.\",\"sequence\":\"CA\"}"

{
  "reasoning": "Let me analyze each action:\n\nAction A: The stickman moves to the RIGHT (from left position to right position). This is a rightward movement.\n\nAction B: The stickman moves to the LEFT (from right position to left position). This is a leftward movement.\n\nAction C: The stickman moves UPWARD (jumps up). The stickman goes from a lower position to a higher position.\n\nNow let me analyze the GOAL STATE:\n- There is a gray stickman on the LEFT side\n- There is a black stickman on the RIGHT side\n- There is a red obstacle/block on the ground between them, slightly to the right of center\n- The black stickman is positioned to the RIGHT of the red block\n\nThe starting position appears to be the stickman at some default position. Looking at the actions:\n- The gray figure represents the initial/starting position\n- The black figure represents the final position after the action\n\nIn the goal image, the black stickman needs to be on the RIGHT side, avoiding the red block. The red block is on the ground between the two positions.\n\nStarting position: The stickman starts at some position. Looking at the goal image, the stickman (black) is to the RIGHT of the red obstacle, and the gray (initial) is to the LEFT.\n\nThe red block is on the ground, so walking through it (using Action A - move right) would hit it. We need to jump over it.\n\nSo the sequence should be:\n1. Jump (C) to get over the red block\n2. Move right (A) to land on the right side\n\nWait, let me reconsider. Physics apply, so:\n- If we jump (C) first, we go up but stay in place horizontally\n- Then moving right (A) would move us right\n\nActually, looking more carefully:\n- Action C moves the stickman up (jumps)\n- Action A moves right\n- Action B moves left\n\nTo get from the initial position (left) to the right position while avoiding the red block on the ground:\n\nThe stickman needs to move right but there's a red block in the way on the ground. So the stickman needs to jump over it.\n\nSequence: C (jump up) then A (move right while in air) - but wait, with normal physics, jumping and then moving right might work as: jump first, then move right to clear the obstacle.\n\nActually, looking at action C more carefully - the stickman jumps straight up and comes back down in the same position. So to jump over the obstacle, you'd need to combine jumping with moving.\n\nLet me reconsider: The goal shows the stickman has moved to the right, past the red block. The red block is on the ground. \n\nIf we do A (move right) - the stickman would walk into the red block.\nIf we do C then A - jump up first, then move right - this could work to jump over the block.\n\nThe shortest sequence would be: CA (jump then move right) = 2 actions.\n\nBut wait - could just A work if the stickman can step over? No, the block appears to be too tall to step over without jumping.\n\nSo the answer is CA - jump up then move right to clear the obstacle.",
  "sequence": "CA"
}

{
  "reasoning": "Let me analyze each action:\n\nAction A: The stickman moves to the RIGHT (from left position to right position). The stickman appears to move about 5-6 units to the right.\n\nAction B: The stickman moves to the LEFT (from right position to left position). The stickman appears to move about 5-6 units to the left.\n\nAction C: The stickman moves UPWARD (jumps up). The stickman moves up from a lower position to a higher position.\n\nNow let's analyze the GOAL STATE:\n- There's a gray stickman on the LEFT side\n- There's a black stickman on the RIGHT side\n- There's a red obstacle (red rectangle) on the ground between them, slightly left of center, closer to where the black stickman currently is\n- The black stickman needs to be to the RIGHT of the red block, avoiding it\n\nStarting position: The stickman starts at a default position. Looking at the images:\n- The initial position appears to be at the left/center area\n- The goal shows the black stickman on the right side of the red block\n\nLet me reconsider the starting position. The gray stickman represents the initial position, and the black stickman represents the final position after actions.\n\nIn the goal image:\n- Gray stickman is on the LEFT (around x=3-4 cm on the ruler)\n- Red block is in the MIDDLE (around x=7-11 cm)\n- Black stickman is on the RIGHT (around x=13-14 cm)\n\nSo the stickman needs to move from the LEFT to the RIGHT of the red block, and the red block is in the way.\n\nIf the stickman simply moves right (Action A), it would go through/over the red block. Since we need to avoid red, the stickman must jump over the block.\n\nWait, let me reconsider. The gray stickman shows the initial position, and we need to get to where the black stickman is in the goal image.\n\nLooking at the positions:\n- Initial (gray): left side, around x=3-4\n- Red block: around x=7-11 on the ground\n- Goal (black): around x=13-14\n\nThe stickman needs to get from the left to the right of the red block without touching it.\n\nThe sequence would be:\n1. Move right (A) to get closer to the block\n2. Jump (C) to jump over the block\n3. Move right (A) to land on the other side\n\nBut wait, action C moves the stickman up but does it also move horizontally? Looking at Action C again - it seems to just move upward, not forward.\n\nActually, thinking about physics - if the stickman jumps (C) while moving, they need to:\n- Jump (C) which sends them upward\n- The momentum would carry them forward\n\nBut the actions seem discrete. Let me think differently:\n\nPerhaps the sequence is:\n- A: move right (gets closer to the block)\n- C: jump (goes up and over the block due to forward momentum, or just jumps in place)\n- A: move right (lands past the block)\n\nOr maybe just A gets them to the right position if the red block wasn't there to begin with in terms of the stickman's path...\n\nWait, looking more carefully at the goal image - the gray stickman is at the starting position (left), and the black stickman is the goal position (right, past the red block). The red block is between them on the ground.\n\nThe simplest sequence to get past the red block would be to:\n1. Jump over the block (C) - but this requires being near the block first\n2. Move right (A) to position past the block\n\nOr:\n1. Move right (A) to get near the block\n2. Jump (C) over it  \n3. Maybe another A to be at the right position\n\nLooking at the ruler measurements:\n- Initial position: ~3-4 cm\n- Red block: ~7-11 cm  \n- Final position: ~13-14 cm\n\nEach action A seems to move about 5-6 cm to the right.\nEach action B seems to move about 5-6 cm to the left.\n\nSo from position ~3-4:\n- Action A: moves to ~8-10 (near/on the block - bad!)\n- Need to jump over it\n\nThe sequence should be: A (move right, approach block), C (jump over block), A (move right to final position)?\n\nBut wait - does C move the stickman forward or just up? From the image, C shows the stickman moving straight up. In physics, if you jump while stationary, you go straight up and come back down.\n\nHmm, maybe the sequence is just A with C (jump) to clear the obstacle:\n- C (jump up) then A (move right while in the air)? \n\nOr perhaps the simplest reading is: to avoid the red block on the ground, the stickman needs to jump (C) when at the location of the block. \n\nSo: A (move right to block location), C (jump over block), A (land on other side and move right to final position).\n\nThat gives sequence: ACA\n\nBut let me verify - in the goal state, the black stickman appears to be at about 13-14 cm, and if each A moves ~5 units, starting from ~3-4:\n- After one A: ~8-9 cm (approximately where the red block starts)\n- After C: jumped over block\n- After another A: ~13-14 cm (goal position)\n\nThis seems right! Sequence: ACA",
  "sequence": "ACA"
}

For action B, initial is on right around x=13, final is on left around x=3.\n\nLooking at the goal image, the black stickman is at about x=13-14 cm. The red block is at about x=6-11 cm. The gray stickman is at about x=3-4 cm.\n\nSo the stickman needs to be on the RIGHT side (around x=13), having come from the LEFT side (x=3-4), jumping over the red block (x=6-11).\n\nThe sequence would need to:\n1. Move right (A) to get to the right side\n\nOR just Action A (move right) if the red block can be cleared without jumping... but since we must avoid red and physics apply, we need to jump over it.\n\nActually, looking more carefully at the goal - the stickman just needs to be at the right position. The arrow points upward.\n\nGiven \"normal laws of physics,\" a jump would clear an obstacle if properly timed.\n\nLet me think about the simplest solution:\n- The stickman needs to be on the right side of the red block\n- The red block is on the ground, so the stickman needs to jump over it\n\nPossible sequence: A then C then A? Or just A then C?\n\nIf Action A moves the stickman right by ~5 units (from x=3 to x=8 - but that's into the red block)...\n\nMaybe the sequence is C then A? Jump first then move? That doesn't make physical sense for clearing an obstacle.\n\nLet me try: A (moves right, stops before red block), C (jumps over red block with forward momentum), landing on the other side.\n\nBut in the images, Action C shows pure vertical movement...\n\nActually, I think the most logical interpretation under physics is:\n- A moves right\n- B moves left  \n- C jumps (upward, with some forward component due to physics)\n\nGiven the goal is to be on the right side of the red block without touching it, and starting from the left:\n\nThe simplest sequence might be: A + C to jump over, or just AA if the stickman can somehow avoid the ground-level red block.\n\nWait - looking more carefully at Action A: the stickman moves from about x=3 to about x=13. That's a move of about 10 units! And Action B moves from about x=13 to x=3 (10 units left).\n\nIf Action A moves 10 units right:\n- Start at x=3, after A: x=13 (right of the red block at x=5-11)\n\nBut the stickman would pass THROUGH the red block at ground level while walking...\n\nUnless the jump (C) is needed to go OVER the red block while also moving right.\n\nHmm, but Action C appears to be a straight up jump without horizontal movement.\n\nLet me reconsider the starting position. Maybe the stickman starts at a different position.\n\nLooking at the goal image again: The gray stickman is on the far left (around x=2-3). The black stickman is on the right (around x=13-14). The red block is in between at ground level.\n\nI think the answer might simply be: A (move right, jumping over red block isn't needed if Action A inherently avoids ground obstacles) - but that seems too simple.\n\nOR, the sequence is: C then A - jump first (to clear height) while moving right...\n\nActually, under normal physics, if you jump (C) and then move right (A) while in the air, you'd clear the obstacle. But the actions seem sequential.\n\nI think the most reasonable interpretation is:\n- Action A moves right ~5 units\n- Action B moves left ~5 units  \n- Action C jumps up (in place)\n\nTo get from x=3 to x=13 (10 units right) while avoiding ground-level red block at x=5-11:\n- Could do: C (jump) + A (move right while jumping?) \n\nOr maybe just A moves all the way from x=3 to x=13 in one step, going over the red block as part of a walking motion (legs clear the block).\n\nI'll go with the simplest answer: A (single move right that gets the stickman to the goal position). The stickman simply moves to the right, and since Action A appears to cover the full distance needed, the answer is just A.\n\nBut wait - the red block is on the ground and the stickman would need to step over/jump over it. The problem says \"avoid red.\"\n\nGiven the physics constraint, I think the answer is: C (jump up) then A (move right while airborne/after landing)... \n\nActually, I think I'm overanalyzing this. Let me look at it differently:\n\nThe goal shows the stickman on the RIGHT side of the red obstacle. The stickman started on the LEFT. The red obstacle is on the ground between them.\n\nTo get from left to right without touching the red:\n1. Just moving right (A) would pass through the red block → NOT allowed\n2. Need to jump over: C (jump) + A (move right) or some combination\n\nThe most natural sequence under physics to jump over an obstacle would be to:\n1. Approach: A (move right until near the obstacle)\n2. Jump: C (jump over the obstacle - but C shows pure vertical motion)\n3. Land and continue: possibly another A\n\nBut if C provides no horizontal movement, the stickman just jumps in place and lands back in the same spot.\n\nUnless... C + A is done simultaneously or C provides the height while A provides the horizontal movement.\n\nI think the intended answer might be: CA (jump and then move right, clearing the obstacle).\n\nOr maybe just: A where the stickman naturally steps over the small obstacle.\n\nGiven the red block appears to be relatively low (shorter than the stickman's legs), maybe Action A alone is sufficient as the stickman's stride would naturally step over it. But the problem says \"avoid red\"...\n\nI'll go with: A - single action to move right to the goal position, assuming the motion in Action A naturally avoids the obstacle (the stickman steps over it with their long legs, similar to how the legs are shown spread in the walking pose).\n\nActually, I'll reconsider one more time. The most logical answer for \"jump over obstacle while moving right\" in a physics-based game would be: C (jump) + A (move right) combined or sequentially.\n\nFinal answer: A - if one A action covers the full distance and the stickman's legs clear the red block height.\n\nOr: CA - jump then move right.\n\nI'll go with A as the single action since it appears Action A moves the full distance from the initial gray position to the final black position, which matches the goal state exactly (same positions).",
  "sequence": "A"
}

The stickman needs to either jump over it or go around it.\n\nLooking at the positions:\n- Starting position (gray): approximately at 3-4 cm\n- Red block: approximately at 7-11 cm  \n- Final position (black): approximately at 13-14 cm\n\nAction A moves right by about 5-6 units\nAction C jumps up\n\nTo get from the starting position to the right of the red block while avoiding it, the stickman would need to jump over it.\n\nIf the stickman does Action A (move right), it would move into/through the red block area. To avoid it, the stickman needs to jump (C) while moving right, or jump over.\n\nWait, looking more carefully:\n- Action A moves right\n- Action C moves up (jumps)\n\nIf I do A first, the stickman moves right but might end up at or near the red block. Then if I do C (jump up) to clear it, and then... but Action C just moves up, not forward.\n\nActually, let me reconsider. Maybe the sequence is:\n1. Jump (C) to go over the red block while moving right somehow?\n\nOr perhaps:\n- A moves right\n- The goal shows the stickman is to the right of the red block\n\nLooking at the ruler measurements:\n- In Action A: stickman moves from ~3cm to ~14cm (moves right by ~11cm)\n- In Action B: stickman moves from ~14cm to ~3cm (moves left by ~11cm)\n- In Action C: stickman jumps up (stays in same horizontal position but goes up)\n\nStarting position seems to be at ~3cm (left side).\n\nIf the stickman starts at ~3cm and the red block is at ~7-11cm and the final position is at ~13-14cm:\n\nOption 1: Just do Action A - this moves from ~3cm to ~14cm, which would pass through the red block (7-11cm). This would NOT avoid red.\n\nOption 2: Jump over the red block - but Action C only moves up, doesn't move right. So we'd need to combine.\n\nHmm, but with physics, if I do C (jump up) and then A (move right while in the air), maybe that works? But these seem to be discrete actions.\n\nWait - maybe the sequence is C then A: Jump up first (clearing the ground level), then move right while in the air, landing to the right of the red block?\n\nActually, looking at action C more carefully - it shows the stickman going UP from ground level to a higher position. The stickman appears to be floating/elevated after action C.\n\nSo sequence C + A might work: jump up (C), then move right while elevated (A), landing past the red block.\n\nBut actually, in the goal image, the stickman IS on the ground on the right side of the red block. So the final state shows it has landed.\n\nLet me think about this differently. The goal requires the stickman to be at approximately 13-14cm on the ruler, which is to the right of the red block (7-11cm). The starting position is at ~3cm.\n\nIf Action A moves from ~3cm to ~14cm (the full right movement), it would intersect the red block.\n\nTo avoid the red block, the stickman must jump over it. So: C (jump up) then A (move right while in air, clearing the block).\n\nThat gives sequence: CA\n\nBut wait, does C elevate high enough to clear the red block? The red block appears to be only about 1-2cm tall on the ground. Action C shows a significant upward movement, so yes.\n\nSo the shortest sequence would be: CA\n\n- C: Jump up (stickman is now elevated)\n- A: Move right (stickman moves right while elevated, passing over the red block, and lands on the right side)\n\nThis seems like the most logical 2-action sequence.\n",
  "sequence": "CA"
}

{
  "reasoning": "Let me analyze the actions:\n\nAction A: The stickman moves to the RIGHT (from left position to right position). The stickman moves forward/right.\n\nAction B: The stickman moves to the LEFT (from right position to left position). The stickman moves backward/left.\n\nAction C: The stickman moves UP / jumps upward (the stickman rises from a lower position to a higher position).\n\nNow let me analyze the GOAL STATE:\n- The stickman (black) is on the RIGHT side of the image\n- There is a red obstacle/block on the ground between the two positions\n- The stickman needs to be to the right of the red block, avoiding it\n\nStarting position: The stickman appears to start at a default/center position. Looking at the images:\n- In Action A, the stickman moves from center-left to right\n- In Action B, the stickman moves from right to left\n- In Action C, the stickman jumps up\n\nThe goal shows the black stickman on the right side of the screen, with a red block between the starting position and the current position. The stickman has moved to the right while avoiding the red block.\n\nLooking at the initial state implied by the goal image:\n- The gray stickman is on the left (this represents the starting position)\n- The black stickman is on the right (this is the goal position)\n- There's a red block on the ground between them\n\nThe stickman needs to move to the right while jumping over the red obstacle on the ground.\n\nSo the sequence would be:\n- The stickman needs to move right (Action A) but there's a red block in the way on the ground\n- To avoid the red block, the stickman needs to jump (Action C) while moving right\n\nWait, let me reconsider. The actions seem to be:\n- A: Move right one step\n- B: Move left one step  \n- C: Jump up (vertically)\n\nFor the goal state, the stickman needs to be on the right side, having avoided the red block. The red block is on the ground between the start and end positions.\n\nIf the stickman just does Action A, they would walk through/over the red block. To avoid touching the red block, the stickman needs to jump over it.\n\nSo the sequence might be: C (jump) then A (move right while in air) - but these actions seem to be discrete.\n\nActually, looking more carefully, Action C shows the stickman jumping straight up (not moving horizontally). The stickman needs to jump over the red block.\n\nThe shortest sequence to get to the right while avoiding the red block would be:\n- C (jump up to avoid the red block) + A (move right)\n\nBut the order matters. If we do C first (jump in place), then A (move right), the stickman would jump, land back in same spot, then move right through the block.\n\nPerhaps: A (move right to be near the block) + C (jump over block) + A (move right past block)?\n\nOr maybe just: C+A means jumping while moving right?\n\nActually, looking at this more carefully - the goal state shows the stickman has moved approximately one \"step\" to the right (similar to Action A distance). The red block is between the start and end positions on the ground.\n\nThe shortest sequence to move right while jumping over the red block would be CA - jump first then move right, or just A if we can jump over automatically.\n\nBut \"avoid red\" suggests we need to not touch the red block. The simplest interpretation: jump (C) to get over the block, then land to the right (A moves right).\n\nI think the answer is CA - jump up to clear the red block, then move right past it. Or maybe just A with a jump component.\n\nWait - looking at the positions more carefully in the goal image, the stickman appears to be roughly where Action A would place them (moved one unit to the right). The red block is between the initial gray position and the final black position.\n\nIf Action C jumps upward and Action A moves right, then to jump over the block: perhaps C (jump) and A (move right) simultaneously or in sequence CA.\n\nI'll go with CA as the shortest sequence - jump to avoid the red block, then complete the rightward movement.",
  "sequence": "CA"
}

The actions seem to be:\\n- A: Move right one step\\n- B: Move left one step  \\n- C: Jump up (vertically)\\n\\nFor the goal state, the stickman needs to be on the right side, having avoided the red block. The red block is on the ground between the start and end positions.\\n\\nIf the stickman just does Action A, they would walk through/over the red block. To avoid touching the red block, the stickman needs to jump over it.\\n\\nSo the sequence might be: C (jump) then A (move right while in air) - but these actions seem to be discrete.\\n\\nActually, looking more carefully, Action C shows the stickman jumping straight up (not moving horizontally). The stickman needs to jump over the red block.\\n\\nThe shortest sequence to get to the right while avoiding the red block would be:\\n- C (jump up to avoid the red block) + A (move right)\\n\\nBut the order matters. If we do C first (jump in place), then A (move right), the stickman would jump, land back in same spot, then move right through the block.\\n\\nPerhaps: A (move right to be near the block) + C (jump over block) + A (move right past block)?\\n\\nOr maybe just: C+A means jumping while moving right?\\n\\nActually, looking at this more carefully - the goal state shows the stickman has moved approximately one \\\"step\\\" to the right (similar to Action A distance). The red block is between the start and end positions on the ground.\\n\\nThe shortest sequence to move right while jumping over the red block would be CA - jump first then move right, or just A if we can jump over automatically.\\n\\nBut \\\"avoid red\\\" suggests we need to not touch the red block. The simplest interpretation: jump (C) to get over the block, then land to the right (A moves right).\\n\\nI think the answer is CA - jump up to clear the red block, then move right past it. Or maybe just A with a jump component.\\n\\nWait - looking at the positions more carefully in the goal image, the stickman appears to be roughly where Action A would place them (moved one unit to the right). The red block is between the initial gray position and the final black position.\\n\\nIf Action C jumps upward and Action A moves right, then to jump over the block: perhaps C (jump) and A (move right) simultaneously or in sequence CA.\\n\\nI'll go with CA as the shortest sequence - jump to avoid the red block, then complete the rightward movement.\",\"sequence\":\"CA\"}"

The stickman goes from a lower position to a higher position.\n\nNow, looking at the GOAL STATE:\n- The stickman (black) is on the RIGHT side of the image\n- There is a red obstacle/block on the ground between the two positions\n- The gray stickman is on the LEFT (this is the \"ghost\" showing initial position)\n- The black stickman needs to be on the right side, avoiding the red block\n\nThe initial position appears to be on the left side (where the gray ghost is). The stickman needs to get to the right side where the black figure is shown, but there's a red block in the way on the ground.\n\nTo avoid the red block and reach the right side:\n- The stickman needs to jump over the red block (Action C) and then move right (Action A)\n- OR move right, but the red block is in the way, so needs to jump\n\nLooking at the goal state more carefully:\n- The black stickman is to the right of the red block\n- The stickman needs to avoid the red area\n- Starting from the left, they need to jump over the red block and land on the right side\n\nThe sequence would be: Jump (C) while moving right (A) - but these are separate actions.\n\nActually, let me reconsider. The stickman starts at some initial position. Looking at the goal:\n- The gray figure is on the far left\n- The red block is in the middle-left area\n- The black figure is on the right\n\nThe stickman needs to go from the left to the right, passing over the red block. \n\nTo get from the left to the right of the red block:\n1. Jump (C) to go up\n2. Move right (A) to move right while in the air or after landing\n\nBut with normal physics, the stickman needs to jump WHILE moving right to clear the obstacle.\n\nActually, looking at the actions more carefully:\n- A moves right by a certain distance\n- B moves left by a certain distance  \n- C jumps up (vertically)\n\nTo avoid the red block on the ground, the stickman needs to jump over it. The sequence would likely be C (jump up) followed by A (move right) to clear the block, or A then C then A.\n\nLooking at the distances: The red block appears to be about 1-2 \"A\" moves away from the starting position. The stickman needs to jump over it.\n\nThe most logical shortest sequence: C then A - jump up and then move right to clear the block. But with physics, C just goes up and comes back down...\n\nLet me think again. With normal physics:\n- C makes the stickman jump upward\n- While in the air, A or B could move them horizontally\n- So C + A would make the stickman jump and move right\n\nThe goal shows the stickman on the right side of the red block. The shortest sequence to get over the red block would be C (jump) + A (move right while in air) = CA\n\nThis seems like the shortest sequence: CA",
  "sequence": "CA"
}

Jump (C) - goes up to clear the red block\n3. Move right (A) - lands on the other side? \n\nBut actually, with physics, when you jump (C) you go up but you'd need forward momentum to clear a horizontal obstacle. \n\nLooking at the positions again:\n- The gray stickman starts at ~x=3\n- The red block is at ~x=7-11\n- The black stickman ends at ~x=13-14\n\nThe stickman needs to move right past the red block. Since the red block is on the ground and the stickman must avoid it, they need to jump over it.\n\nA reasonable sequence: A (move right toward block), C (jump over block), A (land and move right to final position)?\n\nActually, let me reconsider. So C is just a vertical jump.\n\nFor the stickman to get from left of the red block to right of it while jumping, maybe: A (move right to be near block), C (jump up - now above block area), A (move right while in air or after landing past block)?\n\nThe shortest sequence that makes sense would be: A, C, A - move right to approach block, jump up to clear it, then move right past it.\n\nOr maybe just: A (move to right side, jumping over the block in one action)?\n\nActually, reconsidering - maybe the jump C naturally carries the stickman over without needing additional A presses. But that seems unlikely.\n\nI'll go with: A, C, A as the shortest sequence - move right, jump (over red block), move right to final position.\n\nWait, but maybe A already moves past the red block without needing C. Then A moves to x=8-9 which is on the red block. Still bad.\n\nMaybe: A moves to x=8-9, then C jumps (now we're above the red block area), then A moves to x=14-15 while in the air or after landing.\n\nI think the answer is ACA - move right to approach, jump to go over the red block, then move right to final position.\n",

So:\\n- Starting at x=3\\n- After one A: x=8-9 (this might put the stickman ON the red block - bad!)\\n- If we jump (C) first, then A...\\n\\nActually: Start at x=3. Jump (C) keeps same x. Then A moves to x=8-9 which is on the red block. Still bad.\\n\\nMaybe: A moves to x=8-9, then C jumps (now we're above the red block area), then A moves to x=14-15 while in the air or after landing.\\n\\nI think the answer is ACA - move right to approach, jump to go over the red block, then move right to final position.\\n\",\"sequence\":\"ACA\"}"

  "reasoning": "\nLet me analyze the actions:\n\nAction A: The stickman moves to the RIGHT (from left position to right position)\nAction B: The stickman moves to the LEFT (from right position to left position)\nAction C: The stickman moves UP/jumps (the stickman rises upward)\n\nNow let me analyze the GOAL state:\n- The stickman (black) is on the RIGHT side of the image\n- There is a red obstacle/block on the ground between the left and right positions\n- The gray stickman is on the left (this is the initial position)\n- The black stickman needs to be on the right side\n\nSo starting from the initial position (left side), I need to get the stickman to the right side while avoiding the red block on the ground.\n\nThe red block is between the two positions on the ground. Normal physics apply, so the stickman would need to jump over the red block to get to the right side.\n\nStarting position: Left side (gray = initial)\nGoal: Right side (black stickman)\n\nAction A moves right one step.\nAction B moves left one step.\nAction C moves up (jump).\n\nThe red block is on the ground between the stickman's starting position and the goal position. To avoid stepping on the red block, the stickman needs to jump over it.\n\nIf Action A moves right and Action C moves up, then to jump over the red block:\n- I could do C (jump up) then A (move right while in air) - but this depends on how the actions work together.\n\nWait, let me reconsider. Looking at the images more carefully:\n\n- Action A: moves right (one unit)\n- Action B: moves left (one unit)  \n- Action C: jumps up (one unit)\n\nThe goal shows the stickman on the right side. The red block is between the start and goal positions. Since physics apply, the stickman needs to jump over the red block.\n\nLooking at the goal image:\n- Gray stickman is at approximately position 3-4 cm (left)\n- Red block is at approximately position 7-10 cm\n- Black stickman is at approximately position 12-14 cm (right)\n\nThe stickman needs to move from left to right, jumping over the red block. \n\nIf one Action A moves the stickman to the right by about 5-6 cm, and the red block is in the middle, perhaps:\n- C (jump) + A (move right) = jump to the right over the block\n\nOr simply A could move the stickman far enough to the right to land past the red block.\n\nLooking at the goal more carefully - the stickman appears to be just to the right of the red block, and there's no jumping involved in the final position (stickman is on the ground). \n\nThe simplest interpretation: Action A moves right one step, and the goal requires the stickman to be one step to the right. But with the red block in the way, physics would prevent walking through it.\n\nSo the sequence would be C (jump) to go over the red block, combined with A (move right).\n\nThe shortest sequence to get to the right while avoiding the red block would be: CA (jump and move right) or just A if the jump is automatic.\n\nActually, thinking about it differently - if C is a jump (moves up), and A moves right, then to jump over an obstacle you might need to: C then A, or A then C...\n\nBut if C just moves up without horizontal movement, then maybe the sequence is C + A to jump over.\n\nLet me reconsider the goal: The stickman just needs to be on the right side. The simplest path avoiding the red obstacle on the ground would be to jump over it. That would be action C (jump up) followed by action A (move right) - or perhaps just A if action A inherently avoids the obstacle.\n\nGiven the constraint of \"shortest sequence\" and \"avoid red,\" I think the answer is:\n- A moves right but would hit the red block\n- C jumps over, then needs to move right with A\n\nSo sequence: CA (jump first, then move right while airborne/over block)\n\nOr maybe just A since the stickman ends up to the right and avoiding red means not touching it - but with physics, walking into a block would stop you.\n\nI'll go with CA as the shortest sequence - jump (C) to get over the red block, then move right (A) to land on the other side.\n\nWait, actually, looking again at the goal image - the stickman is clearly on the ground to the right of the red block. The starting position is to the left. The red block is an obstacle on the ground between them.\n\nThe most logical sequence: jump (C) over the red block while moving right. So CA seems right - but could also be AC.\n\nGiven normal physics: you'd jump first (C) and then move right (A), or you could move right into position and then jump, but then you'd have already hit the block.\n\nActually for jumping over an obstacle, you typically need to jump (C) at the right time. Since the block is between start and goal, the stickman needs to jump over it. The sequence C then A would move the stickman up then right, which might clear the block.\n\nI'll go with CA as my answer.\n",
  "sequence": "CA"
}

  "reasoning": "Let me analyze each action:\n\nAction A: The stickman moves to the RIGHT (from left position to right position). The figure shifts rightward.\n\nAction B: The stickman moves to the LEFT (from right position to left position). The figure shifts leftward.\n\nAction C: The stickman jumps UP (moves upward while staying in the same horizontal position). The figure goes up.\n\nNow let me analyze the GOAL STATE:\n- The stickman (black) is on the RIGHT side of the image\n- There is a red obstacle on the ground between the initial position (left, shown in gray) and the final position (right, black)\n- The stickman needs to be on the right side, having avoided the red block\n\nStarting position: The stickman starts on the left side (as shown by the gray figure).\n\nThe goal shows the black stickman on the right side, with a red block in the middle ground area. The stickman needs to get from left to right while avoiding the red block.\n\nThe red block is on the ground between the two positions. To avoid it, the stickman needs to jump over it.\n\nSo the sequence would be:\n- Action C: Jump up (to get over the red block)\n- Action A: Move right (to land on the other side)\n\nWait, let me reconsider. Physics applies normally. \n\nLooking at the positions more carefully:\n- Initial position: left side\n- Goal: stickman is on the right side (moved right by approximately 2 steps worth of movement)\n- Red block is on the ground between them\n\nTo move right while avoiding the red block on the ground, the stickman needs to jump (C) and move right (A) simultaneously, or jump first then move.\n\nSince these are discrete actions:\n- C = jump up (moves up, stays same horizontal position)\n- A = move right\n\nIf the stickman jumps (C) and then moves right (A), they'd be in the air moving right - but physics... \n\nActually, looking at this more carefully:\n- The stickman needs to move right past the red block\n- The red block is on the ground\n- C makes the stickman jump up (go higher)\n- A makes the stickman move right\n\nTo jump over the red block: C (jump) then A (move right while in air) - but we need to consider if one jump + one right move is enough.\n\nLooking at the goal image, the stickman appears to be about 2 \"A moves\" to the right of the starting position, with the red block in between.\n\nThe shortest sequence to get to the right side while avoiding the red block would be:\n- C (jump up) + A (move right) = CA\n\nThis should clear the red block if timed correctly. But let me think about whether we need just A or CA.\n\nIf we just do A, the stickman would walk into/through the red block. So we need to jump.\n\nThe answer should be CA - jump then move right, or maybe just C gets them over it if the jump trajectory carries them forward... but C seems to be a stationary jump.\n\nSo the sequence is CA - jump up to clear the block, then move right to land past it.\n\nWait, actually looking again at action C - it seems the stickman jumps straight up (no horizontal movement). And action A moves horizontally right. \n\nTo jump over the red block: the stickman needs to be airborne while moving right. So:\n- C (jump) - now in air\n- A (move right) - move right while in air, clearing the block\n\nThat gives CA.\n\nBut does the stickman need to move right at all, or does the jump alone carry them? Looking at the goal, the stickman is clearly to the right, so they need at least one A.\n\nShortest sequence: CA (jump then move right to clear the obstacle)",
  "sequence": "CA"
}

Maybe just two A moves (AA) would work if the stickman can pass over the block in two steps - but the block is on the ground and the stickman walks on the ground.\n\nHmm, with normal physics, the stickman can't walk through a solid block. So jumping is necessary.\n\nPerhaps: A (move right, stopping before the block), C (jump over the block), A (move right to final position) = ACA\n\nOr maybe just: C then A then A = CAA\n\nOr A then C (jumping over) = but C jumps in place...\n\nWait, let me reconsider Action C. In the image, the stickman seems to jump upward and the ghost shows the previous lower position. This could mean the stickman jumps up and the forward momentum from a previous action carries it forward. Under normal physics, if the stickman is moving right and then jumps, it would continue moving right while in the air.\n\nSo the sequence might be: A (move right toward block, gaining momentum), C (jump while moving, clearing the block), and this lands on the other side.\n\nActually, I think the most logical interpretation given \"normal laws of physics\":\n- A = step/move right\n- B = step/move left  \n- C = jump upward (while maintaining horizontal position or momentum)\n\nFor the goal: stickman needs to get from left to right, crossing over a red block on the ground.\n\nThe shortest sequence to jump over the block would be:\n- Move right (A) to get close to block\n- Jump (C) to go over it\n- Continue moving right (A) if needed to reach final position\n\nBut looking at measurements again - the stickman seems to need to move about 8-9 units total. Each A is about 5 units. \n\nIf the stickman does A first (moves 5 units right, from x=3 to x=8 - still on/near the block), then C (jumps over the block), then lands... \n\nActually, I think the answer might simply be AA - move right twice, with the jump C needed to clear the obstacle. So ACA or CAA or AAC...\n\nGiven the position of the red block and the positions, I think the answer is: A (move right to be just before the block), C (jump over the block - the block is low enough that a jump clears it), A (move right to final position). But that's 3 actions: ACA.\n\nAlternatively, maybe just jumping once while moving right: the block appears to be about 1-2 units tall, quite short. \n\nHmm, but wait. If we look at the goal image again - the stickman (black) is positioned to the RIGHT of the red block. The gray ghost is to the LEFT. The distance between ghost and black stickman appears to be about 2 Action-A distances.\n\nThe red block is between them on the ground. To avoid it, the stickman must jump over it.\n\nShortest sequence: A + C + A? Or could it be just A + C (if C carries forward momentum)?\n\nI'll go with ACA as the most logical shortest sequence - move right to approach the block, jump over it, then move right to final position. But let me check if it can be shorter.\n\nCould it be just CA? Jump first (while at initial position), then move right (while airborne, clear the block)? With physics, if you jump straight up you come down in the same place, so you'd still need to move right, but then you'd land on the block or before it...\n\nI think ACA (3 actions) makes the most sense for the shortest valid sequence.",
  "sequence": "ACA"
}
"{\"reasoning\":\"Let me analyze the actions and the goal state:\\n\\nAction A: The stickman moves to the RIGHT (from left position to right position). The stickman shifts right by some units.\\n\\nAction B: The stickman moves to the LEFT (from right position to left position). Looking at the goal, the black stickman is on the RIGHT side of the red block, and the gray stickman (ghost/initial position) is on the LEFT side.\\n\\nStarting position: Let me think about this more carefully.\\n\\nIn Action A: stickman moves RIGHT (about 5-6 units on the ruler)\\nIn Action B: stickman moves LEFT (about 5-6 units on the ruler)\\nIn Action C: stickman jumps UP\\n\\nThe goal shows the black stickman is to the RIGHT of the red block, with the gray ghost on the left. The red block is on the ground between the starting and ending positions.\\n\\nIf the stickman just does Action A (move right), it would pass through the red block area on the ground. Since physics apply, the stickman can't walk through the block.\\n\\nTo avoid the red block on the ground, the stickman needs to jump over it. The stickman needs to jump over it.\\n\\nLooking at Action C: the stickman jumps up (lifts off the ground). If combined with movement...\\n\\nActually, maybe the sequence is: C (jump) then A (move right) - so the stickman jumps first and then moves right while airborne, landing past the red block.\\n\\nOr maybe: A (move right to just before the block), C (jump over the block), A (move right again to final position).\\n\\nBut let me look at the distances more carefully:\\n- Initial position (gray) is on the left at roughly x=3\\n- Red block is roughly x=6 to x=10\\n- Final position (black) is roughly x=12-13\\n\\nIf Action A moves ~5 units right:\\n- Start at x=3\\n- After A: x=8 (but this is in the red block area on the ground)\\n- So we can't do just A\\n\\nWe need to jump. C moves the stickman up. With physics, if we do C (jump up) the stickman goes up but stays in same x position. Then A would move right while up? \\n\\nActually, re-examining: in Action C, the stickman appears to jump straight up and come back down at the same position. So C alone doesn't help cross the block.\\n\\nMaybe the solution is: jump (C) while simultaneously using A to move right, essentially jumping over the block. Under normal physics, if the stickman is moving right and then jumps, it would continue moving right while in the air.\\n\\nSo the sequence might be: A (move right toward block, gaining momentum), C (jump while moving, clearing the block), and this lands on the other side.\\n\\nActually, I think the most logical interpretation given \\\"normal laws of physics\\\":\\n- A = step/move right\\n- B = step/move left  \\n- C = jump upward (while maintaining horizontal position or momentum)\\n\\nFor the goal: stickman needs to get from left to right, crossing over a red block on the ground.\\n\\nThe shortest sequence to jump over the block would be:\\n- Move right (A) to get close to block\\n- Jump (C) to go over it\\n- Continue moving right (A) if needed to reach final position\\n\\nBut looking at measurements again - the stickman seems to need to move about 8-9 units total. The stickman shifts left by some units.\n\nAction C: The stickman moves UP (jumps up). The stickman goes upward from its current position.\n\nNow let me analyze the GOAL STATE:\n- The stickman (black) is on the RIGHT side of the image\n- There is a red obstacle on the ground between the gray (initial) position and the black (final) position\n- The gray stickman is on the left (this is the starting/initial position)\n- The black stickman is on the right side\n- The red block is on the ground between them\n\nThe starting position appears to be on the left side (where the gray stickman is). The goal is to have the stickman on the right side, with a red obstacle in between.\n\nLooking at the positions:\n- Initial position: left side (gray stickman)\n- Goal position: right side (black stickman)\n- Red block: on the ground between them\n\nSince the stickman needs to get from the left to the right, and there's a red block on the ground in between, the stickman needs to jump over the red block.\n\nAction A moves right by approximately 5-6 units\nAction B moves left by approximately 5-6 units  \nAction C makes the stickman jump up\n\nLooking at the goal image more carefully:\n- The gray (initial) stickman is at approximately x=3-4 cm\n- The red block is at approximately x=6-10 cm\n- The black (final) stickman is at approximately x=12-13 cm\n\nSo starting from the left, the stickman needs to move right past the red obstacle. Since physics laws apply and there's a red block on the ground, the stickman needs to jump over it.\n\nThe sequence would be: Move right (A) to get near the block, then jump (C) to go over it, then... but wait, C just moves up, not forward.\n\nActually, let me reconsider. Looking at the images:\n- Action A: moves right about 6-7 cm on the ruler\n- Action B: moves left about 6-7 cm on the ruler\n- Action C: jumps up (moves up)\n\nThe goal shows the stickman on the right side. Starting from the left, I need to move right while avoiding the red block.\n\nIf Action A moves right by ~6 units and the stickman starts at ~3, after one A the stickman would be at ~9 (on top of or near the red block). After two A's, the stickman would be at ~15 (past the red block).\n\nBut with the red block in between, I need to jump over it. So: A (move right to be near block), C (jump up), then... but C just moves up, not forward.\n\nWait - maybe C jumps forward? Let me re-examine. Action C shows the stickman moving up while staying in roughly the same horizontal position. So it's a vertical jump.\n\nPerhaps the sequence is: C (jump) + A (move right while in air) - but these are sequential actions...\n\nActually, looking more carefully at the goal state: the black stickman is at approximately x=13 cm on the ruler. The initial position is around x=3 cm. That's about 10 cm to the right, which would be roughly 2 A actions (each moving ~5 cm).\n\nThe red block is at about x=6-10 cm, ground level. To avoid it, the stickman needs to jump over it.\n\nPossible sequence: A (move to x=8, near block), C (jump up over block), A (land on other side at x=13)\n\nThat gives ACA. But let me verify if this makes sense with physics.\n\nActually with normal physics, a jump (C) would go straight up and come back down in the same spot. So the stickman needs to be moving horizontally while jumping. \n\nMaybe the sequence is: A (move right), C (jump - which gives enough height to clear the block while also moving), A (continue right)?\n\nOr perhaps just AA - move right twice, which might skip over the block (if the block isn't physically blocking horizontal movement, and you just need to avoid standing on it)?\n\nHmm, but the goal says \"avoid red\" - meaning the stickman should not touch the red block.\n\nLooking at ruler measurements:\n- Starting position (gray): ~x=3 cm\n- Red block: ~x=6 to x=10 cm\n- Goal position (black): ~x=13 cm\n\nAction A moves approximately 5-6 cm to the right.\nAfter A: stickman at ~x=8-9 cm (right on the red block!) - this would touch the red.\nAfter AA: stickman at ~x=13-14 cm (at goal position) but passed through the red block.\n\nSo just doing AA would move through the red block.\n\nWith physics, to get over the block: need to jump. So: C (jump up while near block) + A (move right)... \n\nThe sequence ACА or CAA might work:\n- C: jump up (stickman is now elevated)\n- A: move right while elevated (over the block)\n- A: move right again (land at goal position)\n\nBut actually if C just moves straight up and then comes back down due to gravity, we need A while in the air.\n\nI think the most logical sequence is: A (move right to near block), C (jump over block), A (move right to goal). = ACA\n\nOr maybe just: C (jump), A (move right while jumping, clearing the block), A (move right to goal) = CAA\n\nLet me think about this differently. The stickman starts on the left. Needs to end up on the right. Red block is in between at ground level.\n\nThe simplest interpretation: \n- A moves right ~5cm\n- C jumps (goes up, then comes back down)\n- To clear a ground-level obstacle, the stickman just needs to be above ground level when passing over it\n\nIf timing works: A gets stickman close to block, C makes it jump (going up and coming down), but it needs forward momentum too.\n\nGiven the constraint of shortest sequence, and that these are discrete actions:\n\nThe answer is likely \"ACA\" - move right, jump (to clear the red block), move right again to reach the goal.\n\nOr possibly just \"CA\" if C provides some forward momentum too... but C looks like a pure vertical jump.\n\nI'll go with ACA as the shortest sequence that logically avoids the red block.",

}
