So B = move left.\n\nAction C: The stickman moves UP. The black (final) is higher than the gray (initial). So C = jump up.\n\nNow the GOAL STATE: \n- There's a gray stickman on the LEFT\n- There's a black stickman on the RIGHT\n- There's a red obstacle (block) on the ground between them, slightly left of center\n- The black stickman is to the RIGHT of the red block\n\nStarting position: The stickman (black) starts somewhere. Looking at the initial setup, the stickman appears to start at a default position. \n\nLooking at the goal: The black stickman is on the right side, and the gray stickman (ghost/initial position) is on the left. The red block is between them on the ground.\n\nThe goal shows the black stickman has moved to the RIGHT relative to its initial position (gray ghost is on the left). The red block is in between.\n\nSo if the stickman starts at the gray position (left), it needs to get to the right side (black position) WITHOUT touching the red block.\n\nThe red block is on the ground between the two positions. To avoid it, the stickman needs to jump over it.\n\nSo the sequence would be:\n- Jump (C) to go up, then move right (A) while in the air, or\n- Move right (A) some steps, then jump (C) over the block, then continue right (A)\n\nLooking at the distances:\n- The gray stickman appears to be at roughly x=3-4 cm\n- The red block appears to be at roughly x=7-11 cm\n- The black stickman appears to be at roughly x=13-14 cm\n\nSo the stickman needs to move right past the red block. Since the block is on the ground, the stickman needs to jump over it.\n\nThe sequence could be: A (move right), C (jump), A (move right while airborne/land past block)\n\nBut let me think about physics. If C makes the stickman jump straight up, then we need to combine with A to move right while jumping.\n\nActually, looking more carefully:\n- Action A moves right by a certain distance\n- Action B moves left by a certain distance  \n- Action C jumps up\n\nTo get from the gray position to the black position while avoiding the red block on the ground:\n\nThe stickman needs to move right (A) and also jump over the block (C). \n\nLooking at the positions more carefully:\n- Starting (gray): approximately at x=3 cm on the ruler\n- Red block: approximately x=7 to x=11 cm\n- Black (goal): approximately x=13 cm\n\nSo the stickman needs to travel right by about 10 cm total. Each action A seems to move about 5-6 cm to the right.\n\nSo: A (move to ~x=8-9, near/over block) - but would land on block. Need to jump.\n\nPerhaps: C (jump), A (move right while jumping) - this would move right while in the air, landing past the block.\n\nOr: A then C then A - move right to near block, jump over, move right to final position.\n\nBut the shortest sequence... \n\nIf C makes the stickman jump and A can be applied during the jump (mid-air movement), then CA or AC might work.\n\nGiven normal physics: the stickman needs to jump and move right simultaneously to clear the block. \n\nThe shortest sequence that achieves the goal (black stickman on right, avoiding red block) would be:\n\n- Start at gray position (left)\n- Need to end at black position (right) without touching red block\n\nIf one A moves the stickman approximately to where the black stickman is (past the red block), but the path goes through the red block on the ground...\n\nThe stickman needs to jump. So C to jump, and A to move right. \n\nShortest: CA or AC\n\nIf the stickman jumps (C) first and then moves right (A) - physics would suggest the jump goes up and the A during/after brings it right.\n\nOr perhaps just A alone doesn't clear the block (moves through it), so we need C to jump over.\n\nI think the answer is: A, C, A - move right (approach block), jump (C), move right again (land on other side).\n\nBut could it be just CA (jump while moving) or AC?\n\nLooking at the ruler measurements more carefully:\n- Gray figure is at about x=3cm\n- Red block spans about x=7 to x=11cm  \n- Black figure is at about x=13cm\n\nEach A action seems to move about 5cm to the right (from the image, the figure moves from ~7cm to ~13cm in Action A).\n\nSo from x=3, one A would bring to x=8 (into the block area). That's not good.\nFrom x=3, C+A might bring to x=8 but elevated (over the block). Then land at x=8... still might be on the block.\n\nHmm, let me reconsider the distance of each action. In Action A, the stickman moves from approximately x=7 to x=13 (about 6 cm).\n\nSo from the gray position (x=3):\n- After one A: x=9 (on top of/past the block but the block is at 7-11)\n- After two A's: x=15 (past the block)\n\nThe issue is that walking through the block (A) would hit the block at ground level.\n\nSo we need: C (jump up) + A (move right) to clear the block, then land.\n\nOr: move right to just before block (but one A moves 6cm which puts us into the block)...\n\nActually wait - maybe C jumps and the stickman stays airborne, then A moves right while airborne (since physics applies, the stickman is in the air), and lands on the other side of the block.\n\nThe shortest sequence would be CA (jump and move right to clear the block) if that puts the stickman at the black position.\n\nFrom x=3, C brings up, A moves right by 6cm to x=9, lands at x=9 which is still on the block (x=7 to 11).\n\nSo maybe: CCA (jump higher/further, then A) or CAA?\n\nLet me try: \n- Start x=3\n- C: jump up (stay at x=3 but elevated)\n- A: move right to x=9 while elevated\n- A: move right to x=15 and land\n\nBut does landing at x=15 match the goal? The black figure appears to be at x=13. \n\nMaybe: CA puts the stickman at x=9 elevated, then lands... \n\nActually I think I'm overcomplicating this. Given the goal position appears to be about 10 cm from start, and each A is ~6cm:\n\nCA: end at x=9 (on block) - bad\nCAA: end at x=15 (past block but too far?)\n\nHmm. "{\"reasoning\":\"Let me analyze each action:\\n\\nAction A: The stickman moves to the right (shifts position to the right). The initial position is on the left, and the final position is more to the right.\\n\\nAction B: The stickman moves to the left (shifts position to the left). The black stickman starts on the left and the gray shows the previous position on the right - wait, let me re-examine. In Action B, the black (final) is on the LEFT and the gray (initial) is on the RIGHT. So Action B moves the stickman to the LEFT.\\n\\nWait, let me reconsider. In Action A: gray (initial) is on the LEFT, black (final) is on the RIGHT. So A = move right.\\n\\nIn Action B: black (final/current) is on the LEFT, gray (initial) is on the RIGHT. So B = move left.\\n\\nAction C: The stickman moves UP. The black (final) is higher than the gray (initial). So C = jump up.\\n\\nNow the GOAL STATE: \\n- There's a gray stickman on the LEFT\\n- There's a black stickman on the RIGHT\\n- There's a red obstacle (block) on the ground between them, slightly left of center\\n- The black stickman is to the RIGHT of the red block\\n\\nStarting position: The stickman (black) starts somewhere. Looking at the initial setup, the stickman appears to start at a default position. \\n\\nLooking at the goal: The black stickman is on the right side, and the gray stickman (ghost/initial position) is on the left. The red block is between them on the ground.\\n\\nThe goal shows the black stickman has moved to the RIGHT relative to its initial position (gray ghost is on the left). The red block is in between.\\n\\nSo if the stickman starts at the gray position (left), it needs to get to the right side (black position) WITHOUT touching the red block.\\n\\nThe red block is on the ground between the two positions. To avoid it, the stickman needs to jump over it.\\n\\nSo the sequence would be:\\n- Jump (C) to go up, then move right (A) while in the air, or\\n- Move right (A) some steps, then jump (C) over the block, then continue right (A)\\n\\nLooking at the distances:\\n- The gray stickman appears to be at roughly x=3-4 cm\\n- The red block appears to be at roughly x=7-11 cm\\n- The black stickman appears to be at roughly x=13-14 cm\\n\\nSo the stickman needs to move right past the red block. Since the block is on the ground, the stickman needs to jump over it.\\n\\nThe sequence could be: A (move right), C (jump), A (move right while airborne/land past block)\\n\\nBut let me think about physics. If C makes the stickman jump straight up, then we need to combine with A to move right while jumping.\\n\\nActually, looking more carefully:\\n- Action A moves right by a certain distance\\n- Action B moves left by a certain distance  \\n- Action C jumps up\\n\\nTo get from the gray position to the black position while avoiding the red block on the ground:\\n\\nThe stickman needs to move right (A) and also jump over the block (C). \\n\\nLooking at the positions more carefully:\\n- Starting (gray): approximately at x=3 cm on the ruler\\n- Red block: approximately x=7 to x=11 cm\\n- Black (goal): approximately x=13 cm\\n\\nSo the stickman needs to travel right by about 10 cm total. Each action A seems to move about 5-6 cm to the right.\\n\\nSo: A (move to ~x=8-9, near/over block) - but would land on block. Need to jump.\\n\\nPerhaps: C (jump), A (move right while jumping) - this would move right while in the air, landing past the block.\\n\\nOr: A then C then A - move right to near block, jump over, move right to final position.\\n\\nBut the shortest sequence... \\n\\nIf C makes the stickman jump and A can be applied during the jump (mid-air movement), then CA or AC might work.\\n\\nGiven normal physics: the stickman needs to jump and move right simultaneously to clear the block. \\n\\nThe shortest sequence that achieves the goal (black stickman on right, avoiding red block) would be:\\n\\n- Start at gray position (left)\\n- Need to end at black position (right) without touching red block\\n\\nIf one A moves the stickman approximately to where the black stickman is (past the red block), but the path goes through the red block on the ground...\\n\\nThe stickman needs to jump. So C to jump, and A to move right. \\n\\nShortest: CA or AC\\n\\nIf the stickman jumps (C) first and then moves right (A) - physics would suggest the jump goes up and the A during/after brings it right.\\n\\nOr perhaps just A alone doesn't clear the block (moves through it), so we need C to jump over.\\n\\nI think the answer is: A, C, A - move right (approach block), jump (C), move right again (land on other side).\\n\\nBut could it be just CA (jump while moving) or AC?\\n\\nLooking at the ruler measurements more carefully:\\n- Gray figure is at about x=3cm\\n- Red block spans about x=7 to x=11cm  \\n- Black figure is at about x=13cm\\n\\nEach A action seems to move about 5cm to the right (from the image, the figure moves from ~7cm to ~13cm in Action A).\\n\\nSo from x=3, one A would bring to x=8 (into the block area). That's not good.\\nFrom x=3, C+A might bring to x=8 but elevated (over the block). Then land at x=8... still might be on the block.\\n\\nHmm, let me reconsider the distance of each action. In Action A, the stickman moves from approximately x=7 to x=13 (about 6 cm).\\n\\nSo from the gray position (x=3):\\n- After one A: x=9 (on top of/past the block but the block is at 7-11)\\n- After two A's: x=15 (past the block)\\n\\nThe issue is that walking through the block (A) would hit the block at ground level.\\n\\nSo we need: C (jump up) + A (move right) to clear the block, then land.\\n\\nOr: move right to just before block (but one A moves 6cm which puts us into the block)...\\n\\nActually wait - maybe C jumps and the stickman stays airborne, then A moves right while airborne (since physics applies, the stickman is in the air), and lands on the other side of the block.\\n\\nThe shortest sequence would be CA (jump and move right to clear the block) if that puts the stickman at the black position.\\n\\nFrom x=3, C brings up, A moves right by 6cm to x=9, lands at x=9 which is still on the block (x=7 to 11).\\n\\nSo maybe: CCA (jump higher/further, then A) or CAA?\\n\\nLet me try: \\n- Start x=3\\n- C: jump up (stay at x=3 but elevated)\\n- A: move right to x=9 while elevated\\n- A: move right to x=15 and land\\n\\nBut does landing at x=15 match the goal? The black figure appears to be at x=13. \\n\\nMaybe: CA puts the stickman at x=9 elevated, then lands... \\n\\nActually I think I'm overcomplicating this. Let me look at the goal image again.\\n\\nThe goal shows:\\n- Gray figure at left (~x=3)\\n- Black figure at right (~x=13)\\n- Red block between them (~x=7-11)\\n\\nThe stickman needs to jump over the red block to get from left to right.\\n\\nThe most logical shortest sequence: Jump (C) and move right (A) simultaneously or in sequence to clear the block.\\n\\nIf we consider that C makes the stickman jump and A moves right, then CAA or ACA might work.\\n\\nActually, I think the simplest interpretation is:\\n- The stickman starts at the default position\\n- Action A moves it RIGHT\\n- The goal position (black) is to the right\\n- There's a red block in between that must be avoided\\n\\nTo avoid the red block on the ground while moving right, the stickman needs to jump (C) over it.\\n\\nThe shortest sequence: C + A = CA (jump up, then move right while in the air to land past the block)\\n\\nBut wait - if C goes straight up and comes back down, then A brings it right, the A would happen after landing (back on ground at same x position), and then it would need to walk through the block.\\n\\nUnder \\\"normal laws of physics\\\": If C is a jump (up and comes back down), then A happening DURING the jump would move it right while airborne. But if they're sequential actions...\\n\\nI'll go with the sequence that makes physical sense:\\n- C: jump straight up\\n- A: move right while airborne (clearing the block)\\n- Land on right side of block\\n\\nThis gives CA as the answer.\\n\\nBut I need to verify the position. If C jumps and A moves right by ~6cm, starting from x=3, landing at x=9 - the block ends at x=11, so still on the block.\\n\\nMaybe: CCA (two jumps? or jump forward?) or CAA?\\n\\nIf A moves 6cm and the block is at x=7-11:\\n- Start: x=3\\n- C (jump): x=3, elevated\\n- A (move right): x=9, still elevated\\n- A (move right): x=15, still elevated, land at x=15\\n\\nThat would work! CAA puts the stickman at x=15, which is past the block (block ends at x=11). The goal black figure is at ~x=13-14, so CAA seems close but maybe a bit far.\\n\\nAlternatively: just A then C then A?\\n- Start: x=3  \\n- A: x=9 (but wait, the path through x=3 to x=9 goes through the block at x=7... but actually the stickman is on the ground walking, so it hits the block starting at x=7)\\n\\nHmm, unless the A action brings it to x=9 and the block is low enough that... no, the red block is clearly an obstacle.\\n\\nActually - I wonder if A action moves the stickman and THEN physics applies (they land). So C+A together: jump, then while airborne move right.\\n\\nI think the answer is CA or CAA. Given the goal position appears to be about 10 cm from start, and each A is ~6cm:\\n\\nCA: end at x=9 (on block) - bad\\nCAA: end at x=15 (past block but too far?)\\n\\nHmm. Maybe the goal only requires ONE A to move right from the starting position, but with C to jump over the block.\\n\\nActually, re-examining Action A more carefully: the figure moves from about x=7cm to x=13cm on the ruler, which is a 6cm shift. But where does the stickman start initially?\\n\\nLet me reconsider the starting position. In the goal image, the gray figure (starting position ghost) is at about x=3-4cm. The black figure (final/goal) is at about x=13-14cm.\\n\\nSo the total displacement is about 10cm. With A moving ~6cm each time, two A's would give 12cm (ending at ~x=15-16), which might be slightly past but close.\\n\\nFor the block: it spans from about x=7 to x=11. Starting at x=3:\\n- After first A (walking 6cm): reach x=9, but this goes THROUGH the block (block starts at x=7).\\n\\nSo the stickman must jump before reaching x=7, or jump after x=11.\\n\\nThe block appears to start at about x=6-7. If we jump first (C), then move right (A) twice, we'd be at x=15 while airborne... but the jump has gravity, so the stickman would arc and land somewhere.\\n\\nI think for simplicity, the answer the puzzle is looking for is: B (move left... wait no).\\n\\nLet me restart with a cleaner analysis:\\n\\nAction A: Move RIGHT (figure moves from left to right position)\\nAction B: Move LEFT (figure moves from right to left position) \\nAction C: Jump UP (figure moves straight up from lower to higher position)\\n\\nGOAL: Black stickman needs to be to the RIGHT of the red block, starting from the LEFT (gray ghost position).\\n\\nThe path from left to right is blocked by the red block at ground level. Therefore, the stickman must jump over it.\\n\\nShortest path: \\n1. Jump (C) to get airborne\\n2. Move right (A) to traverse the horizontal distance while airborne\\n\\nThis is CA = 2 actions.\\n\\nIf one A isn't enough to clear the block, then CAA = 3 actions.\\n\\nGiven the visual, the stickman in the goal is significantly to the right, about twice the width of one A action. So CAA seems more likely.\\n\\nBut wait - in the goal image, the black figure appears to be at about the same position as in Action A's final position (right side of image, around x=13). The gray ghost in the goal is at x=3-4. The starting position for all actions seems consistent.\\n\\nIn Action A alone, the stickman would need to walk through the red block. So the minimum to get to the final position would normally be A, but A is blocked.\\n\\nTo jump over: C then A, but if C+A only takes to x=9 and block ends at x=11... we need CAA.\\n\\nI'll go with CAA as my answer.\",\"sequence\":\"CAA\"}"

In Action A: Gray (initial) is on the left, Black (final) is on the right - so A moves RIGHT.\nIn Action B: Black (current) is on the left, Gray (ghost/previous) is on the right - so B moves LEFT.\nIn Action C: The stickman jumps up - the black is higher than the gray shadow, so C moves UP (jumps).\n\nNow the GOAL state: The black stickman (current position) is on the RIGHT side of the image, and there's a red obstacle in the middle-left area. The gray stickman ghost is on the LEFT side.\n\nStarting position: The stickman starts somewhere. Based on the action images, the stickman seems to start at a default position.\n\nLooking at the goal image: The black stickman is on the right side (around x=13-14 cm), and the gray ghost is on the left (around x=3-4 cm). The red obstacle is in the middle (around x=7-11 cm).\n\nThe goal shows the stickman has moved to the RIGHT relative to the gray ghost position. The red obstacle is between them on the ground.\n\nSince the stickman needs to get to the right side while avoiding the red obstacle, and the red obstacle is on the ground between the initial and final positions, the stickman needs to jump over it.\n\nThe sequence to move right while avoiding the red block:\n- The stickman needs to jump (C) and move right (A) to avoid the red obstacle.\n\nBut what is the starting position? Looking at the default/initial state, the stickman is probably at a standard position.\n\nIn the goal image, the black stickman is on the right side. The gray ghost shows where it came from (left side). There's a red block on the ground between the two positions.\n\nTo go from left to right while avoiding the red block on the ground, the stickman needs to jump over it. \n\nThe sequence would be: jump (C) + move right (A), or move right (A) + jump (C), depending on timing.\n\nActually, looking more carefully:\n- Action A moves the stickman to the right\n- Action C makes the stickman jump up\n\nTo avoid the red block (which is on the ground), the stickman needs to jump over it while moving right.\n\nThe shortest sequence would be: CA (jump then move right) or AC... \n\nBut wait - in action C, the stickman seems to jump straight up (not moving horizontally). And in action A, the stickman moves right.\n\nLooking at the goal more carefully: the black stickman is to the right of the red block and the gray ghost is to the left. The stickman has successfully moved right past the red block.\n\nIf we just do A (move right), the stickman would land on the red block. So we need to jump (C) while also moving right.\n\nThe shortest sequence: perhaps just \"A\" if moving right doesn't interact with the block... but the instruction says \"avoid red.\"\n\nActually, thinking about physics: if the stickman moves right and there's a block in the way, with normal physics, the stickman would be blocked by it or step onto it. To go over it, the stickman needs to jump.\n\nThe sequence C then A would mean: jump up, then while in the air move right... That could work to clear the obstacle.\n\nOr maybe A then C: move right toward the obstacle, then jump over it.\n\nThe shortest sequence to get to the right side while avoiding the red block would be: C + A = \"CA\" (jump first, then move right while in the air, landing on the other side).\n\nActually, I think the answer is just \"A\" if moving right doesn't collide... but the problem says to avoid red, implying we need to jump over it.\n\nLet me reconsider the goal image. The stickman (black) is on the RIGHT side, the red block is in the MIDDLE on the ground, and the gray ghost is on the LEFT. This suggests the stickman successfully navigated from left to right past the red block.\n\nThe shortest sequence that avoids the red block: CA (jump, then move right) - 2 actions.\n\nI'll go with \"CA\" as my answer.",
  "sequence": "CA"
}

Then move right (A) while in the air... but these are discrete actions.\n\nActually, looking at this more carefully:\n- Action A: moves right (one step)\n- Action B: moves left (one step)  \n- Action C: jumps up\n\nTo get from left to right over a red block:\n- The stickman needs to jump over the red block\n\nThe sequence could be: A (move right toward block), C (jump), A (move right while over block/after landing)\n\nBut wait - the goal shows the stickman has moved to the RIGHT of the red block. The simplest path would be to jump (C) and move right (A) simultaneously or in sequence.\n\nActually, with physics, you'd need to: move right to approach, jump to clear the obstacle, and land on the other side.\n\nLooking at the positions more carefully in the goal image - the stickman (black) is fairly far to the right of the red block. The gray ghost is on the far left.\n\nThe shortest sequence to move right while avoiding a ground-level obstacle would be:\n- C (jump up) then A (move right while in air) - this would allow clearing the red block\n\nOr simply A then C then A...\n\nBut the SHORTEST sequence is asked. If one jump (C) is enough to clear the block and land on the right side, then maybe just CA or AC.\n\nLooking at the goal - the black stickman appears to be about 2 \"steps\" to the right of center, and the red block is about 1 step to the right of the gray starting position.\n\nThe simplest interpretation: The stickman needs to jump over the red block. Since the red block is on the ground and the stickman needs to be to the RIGHT of it, the sequence might be:\n- A (move right once, getting close to the block)\n- C (jump over the block)\n- A (move right to land past the block)\n\nThat's ACA - 3 actions.\n\nOr maybe just CA - jump and move right clears it in 2 actions.\n\nHmm, but looking at action C, it seems to just jump straight up, not forward. So we'd need a separate right movement.\n\nLet me reconsider the goal image position. The black stickman is clearly to the RIGHT, and the starting position (gray ghost) is to the LEFT. The red block is between them at ground level.\n\nShortest sequence: The stickman needs to move right but there's a red block on the ground. Jump (C) to go over it, then move right (A). But if C just goes straight up and A moves right, then CA would put the stickman up-right... but with physics, after jumping it comes back down.\n\nI think the answer is: A (move toward block), C (jump over block), A (land on right side) = ACA\n\nBut could it be just CA? If C jumps and A moves right at the same time or right after...\n\nActually the most logical shortest path considering the obstacle is on the ground: jump (C) + move right (A) = CA, landing past the red block on the right side. That seems like the shortest at 2 actions.\n\nWait, but looking at where the stickman starts vs where the red block is... The stickman might need to move right first before jumping. Let me look at the goal image again.\n\nIn the goal: gray ghost is far LEFT, red block is in MIDDLE-LEFT area, black stickman is on the RIGHT. There seems to be some distance between the gray position and the red block, suggesting the stickman moved right first, then jumped over the block.\n\nI'll go with: The stickman starts at the gray position (far left), needs to reach the black position (far right), avoiding the red block in between. \n\nSince Action A moves right and Action C jumps, to cross over the red block:\n- Move right (A) to get near the block\n- Jump (C) over the block  \n- Move right (A) to complete the movement to the right\n\n= ACA (3 actions)\n\nBut maybe the red block is right next to the starting position, so just C then A = CA.\n\nI'll go with CA as the shortest sequence - jump first to clear the red block, then move right to land on the other side.",
  "sequence": "CA"
}
"{\"reasoning\":\"Let me analyze each action:\\n\\nAction A: The stickman moves to the RIGHT (from left position to right position). The stickman shifts rightward.\\n\\nAction B: The stickman moves to the LEFT (from right position to left position). The black stickman is on the left, and the gray (initial) is on the right - so this moves the stickman leftward.\\n\\nWait, let me reconsider. Looking at Action B more carefully: The black stickman (current) is on the LEFT, and the gray stickman (initial/previous position) is on the RIGHT. This means the stickman moved FROM right TO left. So Action B moves the stickman to the LEFT.\\n\\nAction A: Black stickman is on the RIGHT, gray is on the LEFT. So the stickman moved from left to right. Action A moves RIGHT.\\n\\nAction C: The stickman moves UPWARD (jumps). The black stickman is higher than the gray initial position.\\n\\nNow for the GOAL STATE:\\n- The gray stickman (initial position) is on the LEFT side\\n- The black stickman (current position) is on the RIGHT side\\n- There's a red block between them on the ground\\n- We need to avoid red (can't touch the red block)\\n\\nStarting position: The stickman starts somewhere. Looking at the initial setup, the stickman appears to start in a default position.\\n\\nThe goal shows the black stickman on the RIGHT side, with the gray (ghost/initial) on the LEFT. The red block is between them at ground level.\\n\\nSo the stickman needs to move from the left to the right, passing over the red block. \\n\\nTo go from left to right while avoiding the red block on the ground, the stickman would need to:\\n1. Jump (C) to go over the red block\\n2. Move right (A) while in the air or after landing\\n\\nWait, but physics applies. Let me think about this more carefully.\\n\\nThe initial position of the stickman seems to be on the left (where the gray ghost is shown in the goal image). The stickman needs to reach the right side where the black figure is shown, while avoiding the red block in the middle.\\n\\nThe red block is on the ground between the two positions. To avoid it, the stickman needs to jump over it.\\n\\nSo: Jump (C) + Move right (A) = but the order matters. \\n\\nIf we jump first (C), the stickman goes up. Then move right (A) while in the air... but these are discrete actions.\\n\\nActually, looking at this more carefully:\\n- Action A: moves right (one step)\\n- Action B: moves left (one step)  \\n- Action C: jumps up\\n\\nTo get from left to right over a red block:\\n- The stickman needs to jump over the red block\\n\\nThe sequence could be: A (move right toward block), C (jump), A (move right while over block/after landing)\\n\\nBut wait - the goal shows the stickman has moved to the RIGHT of the red block. The simplest path would be to jump (C) and move right (A) simultaneously or in sequence.\\n\\nActually, with physics, you'd need to: move right to approach, jump to clear the obstacle, and land on the other side.\\n\\nLooking at the positions more carefully in the goal image - the stickman (black) is fairly far to the right of the red block. The gray ghost is on the far left.\\n\\nThe shortest sequence to move right while avoiding a ground-level obstacle would be:\\n- C (jump up) then A (move right while in air) - this would allow clearing the red block\\n\\nOr simply A then C then A...\\n\\nBut the SHORTEST sequence is asked. Since the red block is on the ground and the stickman needs to be to the RIGHT of it, the sequence might be:\\n- A (move right once, getting close to the block)\\n- C (jump over the block)\\n- A (move right to land past the block)\\n\\nThat's ACA - 3 actions.\\n\\nOr maybe just CA - jump and move right clears it in 2 actions.\\n\\nHmm, but looking at action C, it seems to just jump straight up, not forward. So we'd need a separate right movement.\\n\\nLet me reconsider the goal image position. The black stickman is clearly to the RIGHT, and the starting position (gray ghost) is to the LEFT. That seems like the shortest at 2 actions.\\n\\nWait, but looking at where the stickman starts vs where the red block is... The stickman might need to move right first before jumping. There seems to be some distance between the gray position and the red block, suggesting the stickman moved right first, then jumped over the block.\\n\\nI'll go with: The stickman starts at the gray position (far left), needs to reach the black position (far right), avoiding the red block in between. \\n\\nSince Action A moves right and Action C jumps, to cross over the red block:\\n- Move right (A) to get near the block\\n- Jump (C) over the block  \\n- Move right (A) to complete the movement to the right\\n\\n= ACA (3 actions)\\n\\nBut maybe the red block is right next to the starting position, so just C then A = CA.\\n\\nI'll go with CA as the shortest sequence - jump first to clear the red block, then move right to land on the other side.\",\"sequence\":\"CA\"}"
